pages.name: Scene 5 · The Friendly Wall — Arthen Moll

text.content:
Scene 5 · The Friendly Wall — Arthen Moll

Trigger this once the party has named at least two of the three (Cindren & Vhal, Gilst, Moraven) and hasn't yet committed to a way east — the afternoon of the evidence review, or the next morning if they were out late. If the chase ran loud and public, feel free to bring Moll a day early on the strength of it.

Arthen Moll. The friendliest obstacle the party will meet in Loftwick.

Who he is

Arthen Moll, Deputy Voice of Civic Reconciliation. He is the friendliest obstacle the party will meet in Loftwick, and the most instructive: he is not corrupt, and he is completely in the conspiracy's way of wanting. The Office of Civic Reconciliation is a Yeomanry administrative body created originally to mediate trade disputes between guilds; under the current Spokesman it has quietly expanded into a softer cousin of the Council's public-closure faction. Moll is its most visible deputy.

The conspirators do not own Moll. They do not need to. They benefit from his sincere belief that drawing attention to financial irregularities damages the Yeomanry's reputation more than it protects its institutions.

How he arrives

By appointment, two clerks and their portfolios in tow, a card sent up ahead. Caelith quietly suggests receiving him in the dense, unglamorous Library rather than the Reception Parlor — harder for a man like Moll to perform in. Trina, if she's about, slips in behind him and takes the chair at the window, and says nothing unless spoken to.

Read-aloud — Moll's pitch

Moll smiles when he sees you — the same warm smile he gave the footman, the same one he'll give the cook on his way out. He doesn't sit until you do. "Friends of the Common Defense," he says, with feeling. "What you have done for the Yeomanry will be remembered for at least a full council session. I'll not flatter you with more than that. I've come on a small errand, which I think you'll find a sensible one.

"The matter you're turning over — the bonded firms, the unfortunate clerk, the missing factor on Tannerway — is, as you've surely seen, a matter of account-keeping. The Yeomanry has institutions for account-keeping. The Treasury, the Audit Hall, the Bureau. Slow, yes. But ours, and older than any of us. The Office of Civic Reconciliation would like to say, formally and with gratitude, that you have done enough. We'd like you to take a small honorarium — twelve hundred gold each, by Yeomanry draft, not Council coin — and a quiet departure to wherever you're next needed. You'll be named in the durable record as the friends who lit the candle. We will, with our slower hands, carry it to its lamp."

He looks pleasantly at each of you in turn. "If you accept, we can have the drafts here by sundown. If you don't — and I say this because I respect you — we will not block your inquiry; the office hasn't the power, and wouldn't use it if it had. We'll only ask you to weigh that visible inquiry by friends-of-the-Yeomanry into an Aerdi-licensed firm has a cost to our trade. The eastern banks watch. They'll read your visibility as policy, and we'll pay for it in basis points for two quarters. That's all. May I leave the drafts, in case you change your minds tonight?"

What's actually on the table

  • The bribe is real. 1,200 gp each, clean money from a real fund. The money is clean; the office is real; the offer is sincere on its face.
  • The threat is institutional, not violent. Moll can't stop the party, but he can file a note with the Council that costs Caelith political capital, and he will. Council members of his faction will then begin asking Caelith uncomfortable questions in private session.
  • He believes every word. He is the wall the soft-pressure faction puts up before things get loud — and the party's own visible legwork is what summoned him.

Insight checks

  • Insight DC 12: Moll is sincere. He does not enjoy delivering this errand, but he believes it is the right one.
  • Insight DC 15: the line about "the eastern banks watch" isn't his own — he's repeating someone else's argument as his own.
  • Insight DC 18: the whole speech is a script he has run three times this season, on three different matters. He is the wall the soft-pressure faction puts up before things get loud.

The decision and its consequences

Decline (the path the chapter expects)

Moll smiles, gathers his drafts, and leaves the door open behind him. Caelith, told of the refusal, is visibly relieved and doesn't say so. Advance the Pressure Clock one segment — and within a day, Cindren & Vhal (via Vesh's earlier message) confirm the party won't be administratively contained, and escalate. Merev Sarth comes the following night (Scene 6).

Defer ("we'll think about it")

He leaves the drafts and returns next morning. Advance the clock anyway; Sarth comes the night the party finally decides.

Accept

Let them. Run a session of downtime — spend the honoraria, train, craft. Then, three weeks on, Caelith arrives with the bill the party didn't pay: Tamsin's body has surfaced in the Sheldomar, Vellin and Ailen are gone, Pollow is dead, and Caelith is being recalled. The conspiracy used the time the party wouldn't. Most tables re-engage hard, now owing both Caelith and themselves. (It's a supported path, not a punishment — but the disappointment in the room teaches the lesson better than any lecture.)

How Trina reads him

If the party asks Trina afterward what she made of him, she sets her cup down: "He believes what he says. That is the worst kind of obstacle. You cannot persuade him. You can only outwork him."

pages.name: Scene 6 · The Night Visitor — Merev Sarth

text.content:
Scene 6 · The Night Visitor — Merev Sarth

The night after the party rebuffs Moll, the firm tries the other kind of pressure. Run this tense, not lethal: Sarth is on a retrieval contract, paid extra not to leave bodies, and she runs the instant the job goes wrong.

Merev Sarth, on a retrieval night. Not a kill contract.

Who she is

Merev Sarth is a professional thief — the same "watchman" who searched Gilst's rooms on a reconnaissance pass. She has been paid by Cindren & Vhal Hardby (through a Hardby intermediary) to retrieve or destroy the contents of the Little Palace's cellar strongroom. She has been paid extra not to leave bodies. She will fight only as a last resort, and she will run the moment retrieval becomes impossible. Her fallback is to douse and ignite the evidence rather than allow the party to keep it.

Her plan

Her plan is clean. She comes over the courtyard wall in a real courier's tabard with a forged Audit Hall inspection writ, talks or slips past the gate guard, goes down the spiral stair to the strongroom, picks its double lock (she has a wax cast of the second tumbler, taken by the firm's clerk during a "Yeomanry inspection"), empties the chest into her wallet, and leaves not the way she came but through the old service tunnel in the cellar's east wall. What she burns, if she's cornered, she burns rather than leave behind.

What Sarth doesn't know — and what makes this a trap, not a heist

  • The chest holds decoys. Caelith and Trina expected this the moment Vesh's runner left Wick Lane; the real evidence has been at Trina's townhouse since sundown.
  • The old service tunnel — her clever exit — has been watched for three days. A steady Yeomanry corporal, Brell Kostan, has been posted at its far end since morning.
  • There's a soft alarm rune on the inside of the strongroom door (Caelith's own work; a character inside can spot it for what it is at Arcana DC 14). It chimes in the upper hall and Caelith's study.

How it plays

If anyone in the party is awake and about (a night-owl studying in the library, someone who heard a sound), they can intercept her anywhere on her route — and her priority is exit, never victory: smoke pellet, Disengage, run for the tunnel. If the party is asleep, the rune wakes Caelith, and Caelith and Trina go down with lanterns to find her at the door or mid-transfer; Caelith doesn't draw a blade, he just tells her, evenly, to leave the wallet and take the tunnel as she planned, "and the corporal at the other end will explain what happens next." Recognizing two far more dangerous people than her client warned her of, she drops it and runs — into Kostan.

If it comes to a fight

She's quick and slippery (CR 4 — AC 15, HP 65, Sneak Attack, Uncanny Dodge, Evasion), but the strongroom is a cramped 10-by-12 box and she's there to leave, not to win. She uses smoke to blind the door, not the room. At 30 HP or below she yields; offered the chance to surrender she takes it. Run her as a kill-the-party encounter and you've misread the scene.

What the night is worth

The party keeps every real piece of evidence (it was never in the chest), and they gain a captured thief, a forged Audit Hall seal, and — once Sarth talks — a name. Held lawfully a couple of days and offered Caelith's standing deal (a Yeomanry passport and a hundred gold for one future testimony; Persuasion DC 14 and she takes it, being a contractor and not a fanatic), she gives up that her paymaster was a Hardby intermediary she knows only as "Tarsh" — a thin man in his fifties with a smoker's voice — and that she was paid in laundered drafts drawn on banks that aren't Cindren & Vhal. She will die before naming the broker who connects her to work; don't push it.

Advance the Pressure Clock one segment regardless of how the night ends — the firm now knows neither bribe nor burglary moved the party, and the next push will be worse.

Two threads quietly tighten

The forged Audit Hall seal on Sarth's writ matches a counterfeit stamp Caelith has been hunting in his own office for nine months — the leak, again, brushed and not named. And if the party staked out the drop-niche from the chase (Scene 3d), let it pay off now: they recognize the courier's mark, or anticipate the retrieval, because the watcher's reports and Sarth's contract ran through the same hand. The two squeezes were always one hand. Let that land.
